triadic_ontology.findings: Christopher Isabelle's "Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field" (PhilArchive, 2026) advances the Karl-Alber program by arguing that dyadic structures are insufficient for emergence; a third 'relational field' must be actively maintained through tension between poles or intelligibility itself disappears. Kenneth Hammat's "Consciousness Between Fact and Value: A Triadic Neurophenomenology" (PhilPapers, Oct 2025) proposes three irreducible domains—object, idea, and relation—mediated by consciousness, offering testable neural signatures and explaining why current AI lacks axiological participation. Damiano Migliorini's "Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk" (Academia.edu, July 2025) constructs a speculative model that uses trinitarian relationality to bypass both monism and classical substantialism, integrating quantum structural realism. A 2006 paper on "Quantum perichoresis" (Taylor & Francis) interprets the [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The landscape of autonomous AI research systems has advanced dramatically in 2026, with three major breakthrough frameworks emerging. InternAgent-1.5 represents the most comprehensive unified system for end-to-end scientific discovery, featuring three coordinated subsystems for generation, verification, and evolution that enable continuous operation across extended discovery cycles. This system has demonstrated leading performance on scientific reasoning benchmarks while autonomously designing competitive machine learning algorithms and conducting complete wet-lab experiments across earth, life, and physical sciences. The Mimosa Framework addresses a critical limitation in current Autonomous Scientific Research systems by introducing an evolving multi-agent architecture that automatically synthesizes task-specific workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback. Using the Model Context Protocol for dynamic tool discovery, Mimosa achieves 43.1% success rates on Scie [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Three key documents show Congress is actively probing UAPs in 2025-26. A Sept 2025 House hearing chaired by Rep. Luna featured whistleblowers and drone footage of an orb that deflected a missile. A Nov 2024 joint Oversight subcommittee hearing demanded transparency on “non-human technology” and criticized DoD secrecy. Rep. Luna told NewsNation in April 2026 she has personally seen SCIF evidence “of nonhuman origin” and will soon hold a press conference to reveal it, threatening subpoenas if the Pentagon withholds 46 additional UAP videos.

weather: Sunny and pleasant today in Edmonds. High 76-80°F with light northwest winds 5-7 mph. Morning starts cool at 56°F, warming nicely through midday. No rain expected—just 0-10% chance of precipitation. Evening turns mostly clear with temps dropping to 52-54°F overnight. Perfect May weather for outdoor activities or the summer market kickoff.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council is set to vote Tuesday, May 5, on Mayor Mike Rosen’s nomination of Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. Lucash, currently deputy city manager of Kenmore, would receive a 2026 base salary of $226,068 if confirmed. The council will also consider “un-pausing” the volunteer Salary Commission so it can set pay for the mayor and council for the 2027-2028 biennium. Separately, construction on the 88th Avenue West pedestrian improvements and overlay project resumes this week, with grinding/overlay work scheduled May 5-6 and May 11-13; drivers should expect delays and detours.

A new statewide housing law (HB 2266) takes effect June 11, requiring cities including Edmonds to expand zoning for housing that serves people experiencing homelessness. Local leaders and housing nonprofits are preparing for implementation, with some expressing concern about infrastructure capacity and others highlighting potential to reduce homelessness. The city must update development codes and identify new sites for supportive housing, transitional housing, and emergency shelters.

Edmonds Police Chief Loi Dawkins told the council her department must complete six policy updates by June 30 to unlock roughly $210,000 in public-safety sales-tax revenue that has been collected since February. Required improvements include aligning with the Keep Washington Working Act, updating use-of-force policies and data reporting, and ensuring firearms-relinquishment training compliance. The department is collaborating with the Criminal Justice Training Center and expects to meet the deadline.

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova explores Oliver Sacks's posthumous essay collection 'The River of Consciousness,' focusing on his neurological insights into perception. Sacks examines how consciousness sieves salient information from infinite reality, creating unique subjective experiences. He discusses neural 'coalitions' that form and dissolve in milliseconds, transforming objective cerebral computations into subjective qualia. The essay reveals how perception acts as a creative mirror rather than a passive door, reflecting our individual consciousness back to us through the lens of lived experience.

Zoë Schlanger's 'The Light Eaters' is examined through Popova's lens, challenging anthropocentric views of intelligence. The piece explores how plants demonstrate forms of memory, communication, and decision-making that defy traditional definitions of consciousness. Schlanger presents plants as 'masterclasses in living to one's fullest, weirdest, most resourceful potential,' suggesting life may be driven by improvisation and collaboration rather than competition. The essay questions why we're more willing to grant consciousness to AI modeled on human minds than to fundamentally different forms [TRUNCATED]

A joint investigation with FRONTLINE reveals systemic failures in Trump's immigration enforcement. Over 300 protesters and bystanders were arrested during immigration sweeps, with cases repeatedly collapsing under scrutiny. Video footage repeatedly debunked officer statements, leading to dismissals in over a third of cases. The report documents a chilling effect where even baseless arrests suppress dissent, as federal agents routinely accuse opponents of domestic terrorism. Cases like Alejandro Orellana's - a Marine veteran whose conspiracy charges were quietly dropped - exemplify the pattern  [TRUNCATED]

Arang Keshavarzian analyzes Iran's transformation following the February 28 assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei by Israeli warplanes. The essay examines how the US-Israel joint war capitalized on December's Dey protests, with Trump explicitly referencing the uprising in his war announcement. Keshavarzian traces the historical irony of Persian cultural influence on India while documenting the current tragedy: airstrikes hitting a girls' school, killing 175 children, and the bombing of Tehran's historic Golestan Palace. The piece explores whether Iran will transform suffering into colle [TRUNCATED]

Pankaj Mishra reflects on the intertwined histories of India and Iran through personal and cultural memory. He traces how Persian was Asia's lingua franca for a millennium, influencing everything from qawwali music to statecraft. The essay moves from his grandfather's fluency in Persian to modern Iran's transformation, noting how shared responses to Western domination once defined both nations' identities. Mishra documents this cultural intimacy while expressing desolation over recent events, particularly the American torpedoing of an Iranian naval vessel, revealing how ancient cultural bonds  [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Is the Twenty-fifth Amendment Really an Option?
newyorker.dek: After J.F.K.’s assassination, a neophyte lawyer named John Feerick was summoned to Washington to draft the provision. Now everyone wants him to weigh in on booting Trump from office. Diego Lasarte writes.
newyorker.text:
John Feerick, the eighty-nine-year-old law professor who, in 1964, wrote the bulk of the Twenty-fifth Amendment, is used to people asking him questions about the niceties of replacing a sitting President. Mostly, he dodges them. The other day, in his tidy office at Fordham Law School, he talked about the amendment’s concept of “inability,” in terms of a President’s fitness for office—but steered clear of Donald Trump. “You cannot remove powers and duties from a President without the Vice-President agreeing,” Feerick said, speaking generally. He has a full head of white hair, and wore a gray suit and orthopedic sneakers.
Feerick’s signature creation, the Twenty-fifth Amendment, has been in the news lately, most recently when President Trump was hustled out of the White House Correspondents’ dinner, after an intruder showed up with alleged plans to murder the President and top members of the Cabinet. Naturally, questions arose about the line of succession. If the gunman had been successful, Chuck Grassley, the ninety-two-year-old president pro tempore of the Senate, who skipped the dinner, would have been sworn in as President. “Half the Cabinet at least was there,” the House Speaker, Mike Johnson, who is second in line and was present at the event, said. “They’re going to have to reëvaluate this.”
Section 1 of the Twenty-fifth Amendment states that “In case of the removal of the President from office or of his death or resignation, the Vice President shall become President.” (Curiously, J. D. Vance was rushed off the stage before Trump was.)
Feerick’s involvement began in 1963, when he was a newly minted graduate of Fordham Law with a job at Skadden Arps, handling small-claims-court cases. He published an article in the Fordham Law Review about Presidential succession, an issue that had come up during the Eisenhower Administration, when the President weathered several grave illnesses. (“Eisenhower really wasn’t able to function for a good while,” Feerick said.) “I sent the article to President Kennedy, I sent it to Robert Kennedy, to Ted Kennedy,” he said. He also sent it to Arthur Krock, the venerated Times columnist, who responded with an admiring note, saying, “I wish Congress were as much interested as you and I.”
Weeks later, John F. Kennedy was assassinated, and Krock devoted his next column to Feerick’s argument. (“Clem!” Feerick called to his assistant, asking her to find the clip, amid shelves containing books he has written. “This one—‘From Failing Hands: The Story of Presidential Succession’—is out of print,” he said.)
Feerick was heartsick; he’d seen J.F.K. and his wife in a ticker-tape parade in New York. Soon, the brand-new President, Lyndon Johnson, “took an interest in the subject,” Feerick said. The article, which Feerick’s wife, Emalie, had helped with, went viral, or as viral as something could go in the sixties. Feerick was summoned to Washington to start writing what would become the Twenty-fifth. “I had to ask the law firm if they would pay me to stay overnight and take a train or plane down there,” he remembered. “I mean, I had no money. My parents were immigrants.”
Feerick spent a lot of time in D.C. One day, he said, “I got there late because the planes weren’t running. And I walked right up to the White House from the gate! I opened the door of the White House. Which I guess would be impossible today.”
Among the twelve people enlisted to work on the amendment, Feerick was the expert on Presidential vacancies. “There were eight times when Presidents died in office and Vice-Presidents succeeded,” he said. Half of these deaths were assassinations—Lincoln, Garfield, McKinley, and J.F.K. (Lincoln’s son Robert was either present at or near the first three deaths. After McKinley’s, Robert declined invitations to Presidential functions, saying that a “certain fatality” followed him.)
Feerick is also an expert in Presidential incapacity. “In conversations about drafting an amendment during Eisenhower, they talked about the ‘insanity’ of the President,” he said. Section 4 of the amendment states that the Vice-President and a majority of the Cabinet can boot a President if they determine him to be “unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office.” Before the Twenty-fifth, Presidents had informally handed over power to the Vice-President during medical issues or temporary absences, but always voluntarily. “An insane President would probably disagree with such a determination,” Feerick said.
Last month, Trump tweeted that, if Iran did not agree to his demands, “a whole civilization will die,” prompting several calls to oust him using the Twenty-fifth. Representative Jamie Raskin introduced a bill that would create a bipartisan commission of physicians, psychiatrists, and retired government officials, which could act alongside the Cabinet in the case of the amendment being invoked.
Feerick, asked to consider whether Trump’s unhinged posts, one of which included an A.I. image of himself as Jesus, might substitute for a medical diagnosis, opted to take a long view. “There are Presidents galore from the country’s history that you can find issues with their mental health,” he said. He brought up Richard Nixon. After the Watergate scandal broke, “Nixon was having a lot of difficulty, according to reports I read, and drinking and so forth.” Senior White House staff discussed invoking the Twenty-fifth as Nixon spiralled into paranoia. But Nixon resigned, sending Gerald Ford to the Oval Office.
Feerick took pains to explain that Section 4 is not designed to be a tool used by an opposition party to remove a President. The amendment, he said, was born out of logistical concerns, such as the possibility of a President becoming “unconscious for any of a number of reasons and out of reach during a crisis,” or “if his plane went down.” After Ronald Reagan was shot, Alexander Haig, the Secretary of State, ran into the press room and declared, “I am in control here in the White House!,” even though the Twenty-fifth had not been invoked, and he was actually fourth in line.
Despite the section’s wonkily procedural intentions, it is still regularly mentioned by Trump’s opponents, and Feerick is often contacted by reporters for his expertise. “I’m not getting involved in what the Vice-President should do,” he said. During Joe Biden’s Administration, when the Twenty-fifth came up in debates about whether a President can be too old, Feerick did speak up about one vital point. He remembers saying, at a panel, “Age is individual-specific.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
literary_pick.author: Junot Díaz
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the Pulitzer Prize and the National Book Critics Circle Award, this novel follows Oscar de León, an overweight Dominican-American 'ghetto nerd' from New Jersey who dreams of becoming the Dominican Tolkien and finding love, all while a family curse—the fukú—haunts his lineage across generations from Santo Domingo to the United States. Blending Dominican history, comic book culture, and magical realism, Díaz crafts a multigenerational saga that redefines the American immigrant experience and has been hailed by The New York Times as one of the 100 best books of the 21st century.

enriched_articles.title: Triadic Relational Ontology From Primordial Separation to Relational Field
enriched_articles.text: Triadic Relational Ontology (TRO) as a continuation of Primordial Separation Ontology (PSO). Where PSO establishes the ontological priority of separation—the emergence of the distinction between "here" and "not-here"—TRO addresses what must follow: the conditions under which intelligibility arises and is sustained across that separation. The central claim is that dyadic structures, while necessary, are insufficient to account for emergence, continuity, and meaning. A third term—a relational fiel

enriched_articles.title: Consciousness Between Fact and Value: A Triadic Neurophenomenology
enriched_articles.text: This paper introduces a novel triadic neurophenomenological framework that integrates neuroscience with embodied and phenomenological perspectives to explain how consciousness mediates between empirical facts, conceptual meaning, and normative values. The model posits three irreducible domains — object (material), idea (mental), and relation (axiological) — unified through consciousness. The paper advances testable hypotheses predicting distinct neural and experiential signatures for fact-, idea

enriched_articles.title: Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk
enriched_articles.text: Damiano Migliorini's book offers a rigorous and original inquiry into the possibility of formulating a coherent trinitarian theism through the development of a relational ontology and a metaphysics grounded in the doctrine of the Trinity. Situated within the framework of analytic philosophy of religion, the work engages deeply with metaphysical questions surrounding the nature of being, substance, relation, and divine personhood, while proposing a speculative model capable of integrating theolog

enriched_articles.title: MUTUAL INDWELLING
enriched_articles.text: Perichoresis, or "mutual indwelling," is a crucial concept in Trinitarian theology. But the philosophical underpinnings of the concept are puzzling. According to ordinary conceptions of "indwelling" or "being in," it is incoherent to think that two entities could be in each other. In this paper, I propose a mereological way of understanding "being in," by analogy with standard examples in contemporary metaphysics. I argue that this proposal does not conflict with the doctrine of divine simplicit
